data.title: Adamas Trust stock surges after Q1 earnings beat and book value rises
data.description:
Adamas Trust (ADAM) stock soared 19% in Thursday midday trading after the mortgage REIT's Q1 earnings beat the consensus by a wide margin, helped by gains from derivatives.

"This was a strong result relative to both agency and non-agency mREIT peers that broadly saw book value declines in the quarter," said BTIG analyst Douglas Harter.

Q1 EPS available for distribution of $0.29 rose 26% Q/Q and 45% Y/Y, topping the average analyst estimate of $0.23.

Adjusted net interest income of $48.2M improved 3.9% Q/Q.

Net gains on derivative investments were $87.8M, up from $28.5M in the previous quarter and -$46.8M in the year-ago period. Total income from equity investments turned positive in the quarter to $721,000 from -$3.74M in Q4 but dropped from $3.59M in last year's Q1.

The company also posted $51.8M in gains on the sale of real estate, up from $8.91M in the prior quarter and -$45,000 in the year-ago period.

Adjusted book value per share of $10.80 rose from $10.63 in the prior quarter. Quarterly economic return on adjusted book value was 3.76%

Its investment portfolio totaled $10.9B, increasing from $10.5B in Q4 2025 and $8.3B in Q1 2025.

"Our diversified strategy, pairing agency RMBS with a growing credit and origination business, has performed as designed, generating stable book value and expanded earnings," said CEO Jason Serrano. "We also saw meaningful contribution and operating leverage from our Constructive platform during the quarter."

The  company pointed out that its stock price has significant upside potential as it's discounted by 32% from its adjusted book value.

data.title: Pharma stocks rebound on cheap valuations, patent cliff fears ease, UBS says—CNBC interview
data.description:
Pharmaceutical stocks are making a comeback as valuations remain attractive and companies successfully address patent cliff concerns, according to UBS global head of biotech research Michael Yee.

In an interview with CNBC, Yee highlighted Eli Lilly (LLY) and Merck (MRK) as top picks in the sector, noting that both stocks are trading at compelling multiples despite strong fundamentals.

Yee expressed particular enthusiasm for Eli Lilly, which beat estimates on both the top and bottom lines in its latest earnings report.

“Going forward for the rest of the year, we’re a buy on Lilly,” Yee said, pointing to the company’s raised guidance projecting $36 billion this year and over $44 billion next year. “Stock is trading at, like, 20 times. That is cheap,” he added.

Merck, which reported a narrower-than-expected loss, also drew praise from the analyst despite facing one of the industry’s biggest patent cliffs with its blockbuster drug Keytruda.

Yee noted that Keytruda is projected to generate $35 billion by 2029 when it goes off patent, with the company potentially losing $20 billion or more of that revenue. However, he believes Merck has strategically positioned itself through acquisitions.

“Merck has done an excellent job of buying up lots of biotech companies, and I predict over the next couple years, you’re gonna see this stock a lot higher, like a 140, 150, because they have basically replaced a lot of that patent cliff on Keytruda with 10 other biotech drugs,” Yee explained. The stock currently trades at just 12 times earnings.

The analyst pointed to broader tailwinds for the pharmaceutical sector, including drug pricing deals reached in the fourth quarter and a shift away from AI-focused investing.

“No one’s owned these stocks ex-Lilly for the last couple of years,” Yee observed. “Given I think there’s a lot of uncertainty around the market, the pharmaceutical stocks are having to come back.”

Beyond Lilly and Merck, Yee also highlighted Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) and Gilead (GILD) as turnaround stories worth watching.

He characterized Bristol-Myers as another undervalued opportunity, with the stock trading at approximately ten times earnings while the company continues executing deals to solve its patent cliff challenges.

data.title: American Water expects Essential merger close by end of Q1 2027 while targeting 8% EPS growth in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Water Works Company, Inc. (AWK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "As we announced yesterday, we started 2026 with financial results that were right on track to achieve our full year earnings guidance, which we are pleased to affirm again this quarter, along with our long-term targets." (CEO, President & Director John Griffith)
  • "Adjusted earnings were $1.01 per share for the quarter" and "We expect to again deliver 8% EPS growth in 2026" while noting execution across rate cases and infrastructure investment. (CEO, President & Director Griffith)
  • "Our Board of Directors approved an increase in the company's quarterly cash dividend of 8.2% to $0.8950 per share" and the company "continue[s] to expect to grow our dividend at 7% to 9% per year" aligned with the EPS growth target. (CEO, President & Director Griffith)
  • "Last week, we received our first state approval for the merger in Kentucky" and "we continue to expect the merger to close by the end of the first quarter 2027." (CEO, President & Director Griffith)
  • "As you would expect, the majority of our EPS growth will occur in the second half of the year with revenue increases in key states expected to go into effect in Q3." (Executive VP & CFO David Bowler)
  • "We continue to be well positioned for growth through acquisitions across many states with 105,000 customer connections currently under agreement from deals totaling $565 million" and "we've received approval from the regulatory commissions in 7 of the 8 required states" for Nexus, with closing expected "by June 30." (Executive VP & COO Cheryl Norton)

Outlook

  • "Yesterday, we affirmed our 2026 adjusted EPS guidance range of $6.02 to $6.12 per share." (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)
  • "This represents our expectation of 8% EPS growth in 2026 compared to 2025" and "we also continue to expect to achieve consistent EPS and dividend growth well within the 7% to 9% range through 2030 and beyond." (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)
  • Compared with last quarter, management again affirmed the same 2026 EPS guidance range and reiterated the same long-term 7% to 9% growth framing. (CEO, President & Director Griffith; Executive VP & CFO Bowler)

Financial Results

  • "Consolidated earnings were $1.01 per share, which, as John noted, is in line with our expectations." (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)
  • "Revenues were higher due to authorized rate increases to recover investments across our states, while O&M, depreciation and financing costs increased as expected" and "Our outlook for these categories for the year remains unchanged." (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)
  • "Our total debt-to-capital ratio as of March 31 was 58%" following "the repayment of the $795 million HOS note in February" and the company issued "$700 million at 5.2%" on April 1. (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)
  • "We filed for a refund for the '24 returns, about $84 million" and "there will be a, I'd say, a meaningful cash benefit" from CAMT changes, including that prior assumptions were "about $100 million a year" of CAMT payments. (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)

Q&A

  • Aidan Kelly, JPMorgan: Asked if Pennsylvania ROE assumptions could "bounce back" as results skew to the second half; CEO Griffith responded, "We certainly feel good about the merits of our case in Pennsylvania" and said there is "broad understanding" of investment needs including "PFAS remediation, lead and copper." (CEO, President & Director Griffith)
  • Aidan Kelly, JPMorgan: Asked what merger approvals are required; CEO Griffith said, "We need approvals in all of the states where approvals are required" and "you need all of the required approvals before we can close the transaction." (CEO, President & Director Griffith)
  • Paul Zimbardo, Jefferies: Asked about affordability and stakeholder tone in Pennsylvania; CEO Griffith said the company is "very active" with stakeholders and sees "a lot of alignment" around "affordability and also investment." (CEO, President & Director Griffith)
  • Paul Zimbardo, Jefferies: Asked CAMT impact; CFO Bowler answered, "We filed for a refund...about $84 million" and clarified ongoing impact as "about $100 million a year." (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)
  • Andrew Kadavy, Wells Fargo: Asked what customer benefits are being highlighted for the Essential merger; CEO Griffith said filings are underway and "we do feel like there's good broad support" while emphasizing "affordability and top-tier customer service." (CEO, President & Director Griffith)
  • Andrew Kadavy, Wells Fargo: Asked financing timing; CFO Bowler said the company already issued "$700 million" on April 1, expects equity forward proceeds "around midyear," and another long-term issuance "Q3, early Q4." (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)
  • Aditya Gandhi, Wolfe: Asked why Pennsylvania did not settle and confidence in outcomes; CEO Griffith said, "we do feel good about our prospects for getting a balanced outcome" and described rate cases as "a combination of financial issues, policy issues." (CEO, President & Director Griffith)
  • Aditya Gandhi, Wolfe: Asked whether CAMT cash benefits could reduce equity needs; CFO Bowler said, "we will incorporate the change into the plan when we refresh in Q3, and we'll evaluate the need at that time on equity." (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative to neutral analyst tone, concentrated on Pennsylvania rate outcomes/affordability scrutiny, merger approval certainty, and whether CAMT cash benefits could change equity needs, with repeated follow-ups on Pennsylvania and technical cash-flow impacts. (Aidan Kelly, JPMorgan; Paul Zimbardo, Jefferies; Aditya Gandhi, Wolfe)
  • Slightly positive management tone, emphasizing confidence and schedule discipline, including "we certainly feel good about the merits of our case in Pennsylvania," "we do feel like there's good broad support" for the merger, and affirming unchanged guidance. (CEO, President & Director Griffith; Executive VP & CFO Bowler)
  • Versus last quarter, the tone remained confident on guidance and the Essential transaction timeline, but the Q&A placed more emphasis on Pennsylvania procedural dynamics and explicit cash-tax implications of CAMT. (CEO, President & Director Griffith; Executive VP & CFO Bowler)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Merger process advanced from last quarter’s focus on filing milestones and shareholder votes to a tangible approval update: "we received our first state approval for the merger in Kentucky." (CEO, President & Director Griffith)
  • Nexus timing tightened versus last quarter: management previously said closing "remains favorable to occur by August 2026" and now said, "we now expect the closing to occur by June 30" after "approval...in 7 of the 8 required states." (Executive VP & COO Norton)
  • Dividend action was a new, explicit capital return update this quarter: "an increase...of 8.2% to $0.8950 per share." (CEO, President & Director Griffith)
  • Pennsylvania rate-case messaging evolved from last quarter’s openness to settlements to this quarter’s acknowledgment that "settlement wasn't reached" before the procedural deadline, alongside stated confidence in filed positions and timing for "final order...in July" and "new rates effective in August." (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)

Risks and Concerns

  • Regulatory outcomes and affordability pressure remained central, with management highlighting timing and uncertainty around Pennsylvania and New Jersey, including that Q3 rate-effective timing drives the back-half weighting: "the majority of our EPS growth will occur in the second half of the year." (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)
  • Merger completion risk was framed around multi-jurisdiction approval requirements: "We need approvals in all of the states where approvals are required." (CEO, President & Director Griffith)
  • Financing and equity needs remain a watched item, with management reiterating the equity forward settlement and indicating CAMT-related plan changes will be evaluated later: "we'll evaluate the need at that time on equity." (Executive VP & CFO Bowler)

Final Takeaway

Management reiterated that Q1 results and execution were "right on track" with full-year adjusted EPS guidance of $6.02 to $6.12, while flagging that earnings growth is expected to be weighted to the second half as key rate increases take effect in Q3. Leadership highlighted an 8.2% dividend increase to $0.8950 per share, a first state approval (Kentucky) for the Essential Utilities merger with the closing timeline still expected by the end of Q1 2027, and a CAMT-related cash benefit including an $84 million refund claim and reduced ongoing CAMT payments versus prior assumptions. Acquisition momentum was positioned around 105,000 connections under agreement and an accelerated expectation for the Nexus closing by June 30, with analysts concentrating questions on Pennsylvania rate-case outcomes, affordability dynamics, and whether incremental cash benefits could alter equity needs later in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wayfair slides after narrow revenue beat, profit miss amid choppy home furnishings demand
data.description:
• Wayfair (W) dropped 10% on Thursday after posting first-quarter where it narrowly beat revenue but missed profit.
  • Adjusted EPS came in at $0.26, missing estimates by $0.02, while revenue rose 7.4% to $2.9B, beating forecasts by $10M.
  • The company reported improved profitability, with adjusted EBITDA of $151M and a margin of 5.2%, its strongest first-quarter level in five years. However, it posted a net loss of $105M and negative free cash flow.
  • Wayfair saw modest growth in active customers and higher spending per user, with repeat customers accounting for nearly 80% of orders.
  • CEO Niraj Shah said the company outperformed the broader category despite a “choppy” demand environment, though weak consumer confidence and softer housing trends continue to pose challenges.
  • Stock has lost 38% so far this year. 
  • The furniture retailer faces challenges in expanding its customer base, relying more on promotions amid ongoing macro headwinds such as weak consumer confidence and soft housing demand.

data.title: Caterpillar soars to all-time high after Q1 beat and raise, benefiting from AI buildout
data.description:
Caterpillar (CAT) up 9.2% in Thursday's trading to an all-time intraday high of $889.64 after easily beating expectations for Q1 adjusted earnings and revenue and raising its full-year revenue forecast, helped in part by strong demand for large power generation equipment used in data centers supporting artificial intelligence.

Q1 profit rose to $2.55B, or $5.47/share, from $2B, or $4.20/share, in the year-earlier quarter, and total revenues jumped 22% Y/Y to $17.42B, which the company attributed to higher sales volumes and favorable price realizations.

Q1 revenue from ⁠Caterpillar's (CAT) core construction segment jumped 38% Y/Y to $7.16B, while sales across the power and ⁠energy unit climbed 22% to $7.03B, with both segments helped by strong demand from customers in North America, the company’s biggest market.

Caterpillar (CAT) said it now expects sales to increase at an annual rate of 6%-9% through 2030, after previously guiding for a 5%-to-7% compound annual growth rate.

The company also reduced its outlook for tariff costs to a range of $2.2B-$2.4B in 2026 from a prior forecast of ~$2.6B.

Caterpillar (CAT) is closely watching rising geopolitical tensions and higher energy prices in case recent pressures begin to weigh on demand, but the added uncertainty is not an issue so far, CEO Joe Creed said on the company's earnings conference call.

The AI boom is sparking additional construction activity, which is providing a boost to the company’s other business units, the CEO said.

"A record backlog provides a strong foundation for continued positive momentum," Creed added.

data.title: Semiconductor ETF frenzy builds as SOXL and SOXS investor flows pop
data.description:
Retail trading activity is increasingly concentrating in leveraged semiconductor exchange traded funds, signaling a sharp rise in speculative positioning tied to the chip sector’s near-term outlook.

Recent data from The Kobeissi Letter shows that combined daily volume in the Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3x Shares (SOXL) and Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bear 3x Shares (SOXS) has climbed to roughly 330M shares—its highest level in at least 16 months. Notably, this figure has doubled in just the past two weeks, underscoring a rapid acceleration in participation.

The surge has been particularly pronounced on the bullish side. Trading volume in SOXL ranked higher this week than 99% of all weekly readings over the past five years, reflecting aggressive upside positioning. Meanwhile, bearish counterpart SOXS also saw elevated interest, with activity exceeding 97% of historical observations over the same period. 

In contrast, leveraged products tied to the broader market have seen declining engagement. Combined volume in the Direxion Daily S&P 500 Bull 3x Shares (SPXL) and Bear 3x Shares (SPXS) dropped to around 90M shares, among the lowest levels recorded this year.

The divergence highlights a growing retail focus on semiconductors as volatility and momentum intensify. 

Other semiconductor-focused ETFs: (SMH), (FTXL), (XSD), (USD), (PSI), and (SEMI).

data.title: EU's new Chips Act would let Commission invest directly in facilities: report
data.description:
The EU's new plans to revitalize the semiconductor industry would allow the European Commission to invest directly in manufacturing, Bloomberg News reported, citing people with knowledge of the draft.

The draft of the Chips Act II, expected in late May, is an attempt to improve on the initial European Chips Act, which came into force in September 2023.

The proposal intends to help the EU invest more in key technology and reduce reliance on foreign supply chains. The potential proposal empowers the EC to invest in large, cross-border projects directly. Previously the antitrust regulator was limited to funding research and approving aid from member states. The Commission-backed projects would still be public-private partnerships, the report added.

The new version also intends to boost the development of tech crucial for chipmaking, including machinery, materials, and circuit boards, the report noted.

The European Commission declined to comment in an email to Seeking Alpha.

The rules could still change before they are published and will require  multiple rounds of negotiations between EU governments and members of the EU Parliament before they get approved.

The COVID-19 pandemic showed the risks of Europe’s reliance on global supply chains. The EU brought the Chips Act forward to boost local chip manufacturing and decrease the risk of disruption to local industries. At the time, the EU said incentives for semiconductor projects could reach €86B (about $101B) through a combination of state aid and corporate investments. The measures were intended to make it easier for countries to get authorization to spend money on production and draw in companies willing to build infrastructure in the region.

The objective was to double the EU's market share in global chip manufacturing to 20% by 2030. However, after several proposed projects for chip manufacturing plants were delayed or dropped in the last few years, the European Court of Auditors noted in a report that the target is unlikely to be reached. The ECA’s estimate is that Europe will control about 12% of production by that date, the report added.

In 2024, Intel (INTC) paused construction projects in Poland and Germany. Its planned €30B Dresden facility was on track to be the largest plant supported under the Chips Act.

The Act's put the Commission in charge of just €4.5B, with the remaining funds coming through national governments and chipmakers’ investment. Funding recipients have complained that adding more entities to the process has led to longer procedures and red tape, the report noted.

Update: The European Commission declined to comment.

data.title: Retail equity allocation nears extremes, signals crowded trade, says i3 invest CEO
data.description:
Individual investors’ allocation to equities neared historical extremes, a chart shared by Guilherme Tavares, CEO of i3 Invest, showed in a post on X.

The chart showed allocations, measured as a percentage distance from an exponential growth trendline, rising toward the upper end of the historical range. The latest reading, marked “Now,” sat close to prior peaks, including the dot-com period and late 2021.

In both of those earlier episodes, allocations moved sharply above trend before reversing in the years that followed. The visual also showed extended periods where positioning fell well below trend, followed by gradual recoveries as equity exposure rebuilt over time.

The current level appeared elevated relative to most of the historical distribution, placing it nearer to previous cyclical highs than troughs. The positioning suggested that individual investor participation had already reached levels seen at past turning points.

The comparison implied limited room for further expansion based on historical patterns, with previous peaks followed by declines back toward or below trend.

“This trade is just too crowded. This is not a timing tool but rather a warning that the current situation is not sustainable and that mean reversion is likely over the coming years,” Tavares said in the post.

data.title: Does 'sell in May' strategy actually work?
data.description:
Deutsche Bank strategists are challenging one of Wall Street’s most enduring seasonal rules, arguing that the popular “Sell in May and go away” approach has looked better in hindsight than it has performed as a practical investment strategy.

In a Thursday research note, the bank said the strategy of exiting equities around May and returning around September may appear compelling over long time horizons, particularly in European markets. The strongest version of the trade, selling the STOXX Europe 600 (STOXX) at the end of May and reinvesting at the end of September, generated a 9.0% annualized return since 1987, compared with 7.4% for a basic buy-and-hold approach.

However, Deutsche Bank noted the strategy underperformed buy-and-hold in 25 of the past 39 years, leaving it with a hit rate closer to a coin toss than a durable seasonal anomaly.

The long-run success depends heavily on a few unusually favorable years, particularly 1998, 2001 and 2002, when missing summer equity weakness dramatically boosted returns. Over the past decade, the strategy underperformed in eight of 10 years.

The pattern proved even less convincing in U.S. equities. For the S&P 500 (SP500) (SPY), the cash version of the strategy returned 9.3% annually since 1973, trailing buy-and-hold at 10.4%.

In all, Deutsche Bank found that investors are better served by a fundamental approach than by relying on calendar-based market folklore, regardless of how memorable the phrase may be.
